schools.summary: The self-designation and address: 'the elder (presbyteros — a title of age and apostolic authority, the aged apostle needing no further name) unto the elect (chosen) lady (kyria) and her children, whom I love in the truth (sincerely, in the bond of the gospel truth); and not I only, but also all they that have known the truth' — John writing with love, the truth the bond uniting all believers. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson (with Clarke) hold 'the elect lady' a literal Christian woman, addressed as an individual not a church (the letter speaking of 'her children' and personal in tone, unlike church-language); some taking 'Kyria' (or 'Electa') as her proper name, others as an honorable unnamed lady; Paul uses 'elect' the same way of an individual (Romans 16:13).

Gill (and others) hold 'the elect lady' figurative — a particular church (some suppose Jerusalem) or the church universal addressed under the delicate image of a chosen lady, 'her children' being its members; the language of 'children walking in truth' and the warnings suiting a church, the figurative reading of the recipient as a congregation rather than a single woman.

sides.position: A literal Christian woman — named Kyria/Electa or an honorable unnamed lady, addressed as an individual
A particular church (or the church universal) figuratively addressed under the image of a chosen lady

themes: The elder unto the elect lady and her children, whom I love in the truth
the address to the elect lady
the crux of the elect lady's identity
love in the bond of the truth

schools.summary: The ground of the love just expressed (v.1): 'for the truth's sake, which dwelleth (menousan — abides) in us, and shall be with us for ever' — the love grounded in the truth that dwells in believers (the gospel truth, abiding within, the bond and reason of their mutual love); 'which dwelleth in us' meaning the truth not merely held intellectually but abiding within as a living, indwelling reality (the truth received and lodged in the heart); 'shall be with us for ever' — the indwelling truth not temporary but eternal, abiding forever in those who have received it, the permanence of the truth grounding the permanence of the love it produces (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: for the truth's sake, which dwelleth in us, and shall be with us for ever
the indwelling truth that abides forever
the truth a living, indwelling reality
the eternal ground of Christian love

schools.summary: The apostolic benediction: 'grace be with you, mercy, and peace, from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father, in truth and love' — the threefold blessing of grace (God's favour), mercy (compassion to the needy and guilty), and peace (the resulting tranquility), the full sum of gospel blessing; coming from the Father and the Son joined as the source, the coupling of Christ with the Father and the title 'the Son of the Father' affirming Christ's divine sonship; bestowed and received 'in truth and love' — the two great notes of the epistle framing the benediction, gospel blessing enjoyed in the union of truth and love (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

Cyril: grace, mercy, and peace flow from the Father and from the Son of the Father, enjoyed 'in truth and love' — the title 'Son of the Father' confessing Christ's true and eternal sonship, and truth and love the sphere in which the divine blessing is received and lived.

themes: Grace be with you, mercy, and peace, from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of the Father
in truth and love
the apostolic blessing of grace, mercy, and peace from the Father and the Son
Christ the Son of the Father

schools.summary: The cause of the apostle's joy: 'I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children (some of them) walking in truth (living according to the gospel), as we have received a commandment from the Father' — John greatly glad to find that some of the lady's children were walking in the truth (their faithful, godly walk gladdening the apostle's heart); 'of thy children' (some of them) perhaps simply meaning those he had met, not implying that others were unfaithful (he rejoiced over those he found walking rightly); their walking in truth being 'as we have received a commandment from the Father' — in accordance with God's command, their conduct conforming to the Father's commandment, walking in truth being obedience to the divine command (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: I rejoiced greatly that I found of thy children walking in truth
as we have received a commandment from the Father
John's joy at finding her children walking in truth
walking in truth obedience to God's command

schools.summary: John's tender entreaty: 'and now I beseech (eroto — entreat, ask) thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment unto thee, but that which we had from the beginning (from the first preaching of the gospel), that we love one another' — John appealing to the lady, careful to say he is not laying down a new command (the entreaty gentle, not authoritarian); the command being 'that which we had from the beginning' (not a novelty but the original, foundational command of the gospel, the same love commanded from the start, cf. 1 John 2:7); the substance being mutual love among believers (the great commandment, old yet ever fresh, the heart of his appeal) — Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Henry.

themes: I beseech thee, lady, not as though I wrote a new commandment unto thee
but that which we had from the beginning, that we love one another
the entreaty to love one another, the old commandment
the original gospel command, old yet fresh

schools.summary: The mutual definition of love and obedience: 'and this is love, that we walk after (according to) his commandments. This is the commandment, That, as ye have heard from the beginning, ye should walk in it' — love defined as obedience (to love is to walk in God's commandments, genuine love expressing itself in keeping God's commands, cf. John 14:15); love and obedience mutually defining (the commandment being that they walk in love, to walk in love being to keep the commandment, and the commandment being to walk in love); they should walk in it 'as ye have heard from the beginning' — in accordance with the original teaching, the command to walk in love the same heard from the first, consistency from the beginning marking the true command (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: this is love, that we walk after his commandments
as ye have heard from the beginning, ye should walk in it
love is walking after God's commandments
love and obedience mutually defining

schools.summary: The warning: 'for many deceivers (planoi — those who lead astray) are entered (exelthon — gone out) into the world, who confess not (deny) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh (has truly come as a real man, incarnate). This is a deceiver and an antichrist' — the reason for the emphasis on truth and love being the presence of many deceivers in the world (the false teachers who endanger the church); the deceivers denying the genuine incarnation (the same docetic/gnostic error opposed in 1 John 4:2-3, the denial of Christ's true coming in the flesh); the one who denies the incarnation being both a deceiver (leading others astray) and an antichrist (opposed to Christ), the denial of Christ come in the flesh the antichristian mark (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Henry).

themes: many deceivers are entered into the world, who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh
This is a deceiver and an antichrist
the warning of deceivers who deny Christ come in the flesh
the docetic denial the antichristian mark

schools.summary: The exhortation to vigilance: 'look to yourselves (blepete heautous — take heed, watch), that we lose not those things which we have wrought (the fruits of labour, what has been gained), but that we receive a full (complete) reward' — in view of the deceivers (v.7), the readers to watch lest they lose the spiritual gains they have made (vigilance against being robbed by error); the false teachers threatening to undo the work of grace (the readers could lose what they had attained, the fruits of their faith and labour, if drawn away by the deceivers, the warning against forfeiting spiritual progress); the aim being a full, not diminished, reward (faithful perseverance, guarding against the deceivers, securing the complete reward) — Barnes, Robertson, Wesley.

themes: Look to yourselves, that we lose not those things which we have wrought
but that we receive a full reward
the warning to guard against losing one's reward
vigilance against the deceivers securing the full reward

schools.summary: The doctrinal test: 'whosoever transgresseth (proagon — goes beyond, oversteps, runs ahead), and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ (the teaching about Christ, or which Christ taught), hath not God. He that abideth (remains) in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son' — the one who oversteps and does not remain in the true doctrine of Christ not having God (departure from the doctrine of Christ being departure from God himself); 'transgresseth (runs ahead, goes too far)' describing the false teacher who, claiming advanced or progressive knowledge, oversteps the bounds of Christ's doctrine (such 'progress' apostasy, abiding not advancing beyond marking the faithful); the one who holds fast the true doctrine of Christ possessing both the Father and the Son (to have the true Christ by abiding in his doctrine being to have the Father also, sound doctrine and the possession of God going together) — Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Henry.

themes: Whosoever transgresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God
He that abideth in the doctrine of Christ, he hath both the Father and the Son
abiding in Christ's doctrine is to have the Father and the Son
departure from Christ's doctrine departure from God

schools.summary: The prohibition: 'if there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine (the true doctrine of Christ, v.9), receive him not into your house, neither bid him God speed (chairein — do not greet him, do not wish him success)' — the case of a false teacher (a traveling teacher who denies the incarnation, one who does not bring the true doctrine of Christ) coming to the lady or the church; not to be received into the home (do not lodge or harbour him), concerning not ordinary courtesy to all men but the refusal to host and support one spreading damnable error (to house him being to aid his work); and not even a greeting of approval or wish for his success to be given (the warm greeting would signal endorsement, the believer not by hospitality or greeting to lend countenance to the deceiver) — interpreters noting this is the refusal of fellowship and support, not a license for incivility to all (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley, Henry).

themes: If there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine, receive him not into your house
neither bid him God speed
the prohibition against receiving and greeting the false teacher
refusing fellowship and support to the spreader of error

schools.summary: The ground of v.10: 'for he that biddeth him God speed (greets him, wishes him success) is partaker (koinonei — shares, has fellowship) of his evil deeds' — to greet and endorse the false teacher being to share in his evil works (the one who countenances the deceiver becoming a participant in the harm he does); by encouraging or aiding the false teacher, one becoming complicit in his error and its consequences (the endorsement making one a partner in the evil, neutrality toward soul-destroying error not innocent); to lend support or even approval to those who spread damnable error being to share their guilt (the believer's duty to refuse all countenance to the enemies of Christ's truth, lest he partake of their sin) — Barnes, Robertson, Wesley.

themes: he that biddeth him God speed is partaker of his evil deeds
greeting the false teacher makes one a partaker of his evil
countenancing the deceiver sharing his guilt
neutrality toward error not innocent

schools.summary: The preference for a personal visit: 'having many things to write unto you, I would not (did not wish to) write with paper (chartou — papyrus) and ink: but I trust (hope) to come unto you, and speak face to face (stoma pros stoma — mouth to mouth, in person), that our joy may be full (complete)' — John having much more to say but preferring not to put it all in a letter (the written word not his chosen means for the fuller communication); hoping to visit and speak personally (the personal presence far better than the letter for full communication); the purpose of the visit being that their mutual joy may be complete (the joy of personal fellowship completing what writing cannot, face-to-face communion bringing a fullness of joy the letter cannot give) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: Having many things to write unto you, I would not write with paper and ink
I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face, that our joy may be full
John's wish to speak face to face that joy may be full
personal fellowship completing the joy

schools.summary: The final greeting: 'the children of thy elect (chosen) sister greet (aspazetai — salute) thee. Amen' — the letter closing with greetings from the children of the lady's 'elect sister' (whether a literal sister, whose children send greetings, or, on the figurative reading, the members of a sister church); 'elect sister' paralleling 'elect lady' (v.1), the same interpretive question recurring (on the literal reading the lady's sister, also a Christian; on the figurative reading a sister congregation); the greeting conveying the affection of the wider Christian family, 'Amen' (in some texts) closing the brief letter, the bond of love among believers expressed in mutual greeting fittingly ending the epistle on truth and love (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: The children of thy elect sister greet thee. Amen
the closing greeting from the elect sister's children
elect sister paralleling the elect lady
the bond of love among believers
